risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were randomly assigned to treatment group, stratified by race. Computer randomization occurred after the collection of baseline data." Comment: The study explicitly states that a computer-based randomization method was used, with stratification by race. This is sufficient detail to indicate a proper random sequence generation process.

Quote: "Participants were randomly assigned to treatment group, stratified by race. Computer randomization occurred after the collection of baseline data." Comment: This describes the timing and method of sequence generation, but it does not report any allocation concealment mechanism (e.g. central randomization, pharmacy control, or sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes) to show that the person enrolling participants could not foresee assignments before enrollment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Each diabetes program followed a manualized intervention and included 8 weekly and 2 biweekly 2½ hour group sessions led by trained facilitators. A dietitian led all cohorts of the DSME intervention, and the same dietitian and a social worker with extensive training in mindful meditation co-led all cohorts of the MB-EAT intervention." Comment: The two interventions were clearly different in content and delivery (mindfulness/meditation vs DSME), so participants and personnel delivering treatment would necessarily know group assignment; no blinding is described. Many main outcomes were subjective and self-reported (e.g. food frequency questionnaire, self-efficacy, eating behavior questionnaires, Beck anxiety/depression, mindfulness questionnaire), so knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence outcomes.

Quote: "The valid 110-item Block 2005 Food Frequency Questionnaire ... was self-administered to assess usual intake..."; "An outcome expectations inventory was used..."; "an 18-item diabetes-specific nutrition self-efficacy questionnaire ... was administered..."; "The 21-item Beck Anxiety Inventory... The 21-item Beck Depression Inventory-II..."; "The Five-Facet Mindfulness Questionnaire... was administered..." Comment: Outcomes were assessed largely by patient self-report, so the participant was the outcome assessor for multiple subjective outcomes (dietary intake, outcome expectations, self-efficacy, eating behavior, anxiety, depression, mindfulness). The study compares two distinct behavioral interventions (mindful eating vs diabetes self-management education), and there is no statement that participants or outcome assessors for these subjective measures were blinded. Under the worst-outcome rule, the presence of unblinded/self-reported subjective outcomes makes detection bias high risk, even though weight was measured objectively ("Weight was determined using an electronic scale").

Quote: "Thirty-two participants were randomized to MB-EAT-D; 27 received the allocated intervention and completed data collection. Thirty-six were randomized to SC; 25 received the allocated intervention and completed data collection." Also, Table 2 reports outcomes for "MB-EAT-D (n = 27) and Smart Choices (n = 25)." Comment: Post-randomization attrition on the analysed outcomes was 5/32 (15.6%) in MB-EAT-D versus 11/36 (30.6%) in Smart Choices, a differential loss of about 15 percentage points. This crosses the usual high-risk thresholds (one arm about 30% loss and marked imbalance between arms). Although mixed models were mentioned, the reported analysed group sizes in the outcome tables match completers (27 and 25), so the reported outcome set appears affected by substantial and differential attrition.
